An FDE runs a different operating model. You carry the customer context to the keyboard.

The Philosophy

Build for the problem, not the demo.

Most software fails in deployment. The code is usually fine. The problem is what it was built to solve. The gap between what a customer describes and what they actually need is where projects quietly die.

My approach starts before the first line of code. Understand the environment. Map the actual workflow, the one nobody wrote down. Identify the constraint that everyone works around but no one talks about. Then build directly at that point.

The result is software that gets adopted because it fits.

4 production repos built and shipped at Sphere Entertainment
1 engineer on a full-platform build at one of the largest US school districts
Months → Days data request fulfillment cycle reduced via custom ETL at CCSD
Process

How I Work

Four phases. Each one grounded in real constraints, not ideal conditions.

01

Discovery

Understand the environment before proposing anything. That means talking to the people running the process day to day. I map the real workflow and find where friction lives.

Process mapping Stakeholder interviews Data landscape audit
02

Architecture

Design for real constraints. What's the network environment? What does the auth infrastructure look like? What's the existing data model? I build on top of what's already there.

Schema design API contracts Auth strategy Infra planning
03

Execution

Build fast, build right, iterate with the customer in the loop. Demo early so direction can correct before it's expensive to change. Ship incrementally so each deployment builds on the last.

Iterative delivery Stakeholder demos CI/CD pipelines E2E test coverage
04

Handoff

Real handoff means documentation a new engineer can act on, observability that catches problems before a user does, and a knowledge transfer that actually transfers knowledge.

Technical documentation Observability stack Operational playbook
Value

What Changes When I Join

Framed from the org's perspective.

→

Your team ships internal tooling faster.

I can scope, architect, and build a production-grade internal tool in weeks. Faster feedback loops, earlier adoption, and less time sunk on tools nobody ends up using.

→

Your stakeholders get answers without opening a ticket.

Self-service dashboards, automated reports, and well-structured APIs mean your data team stops being a request queue. Stakeholders pull what they need. The bottleneck disappears.

→

Your systems are observable from day one.

I don't ship without monitoring. Prometheus, Grafana, health endpoints, and structured logging mean you know what's happening in your infrastructure before something breaks.

→

Your technical debt gets documented, not inherited.

Legacy systems I touch get documented. Every decision gets a rationale. A new engineer can navigate what I leave behind in their first week.

Fit

Where I Operate Well

Enterprise Complexity

Large orgs with complex network segmentation, strict auth requirements, legacy infrastructure, and multiple stakeholder groups. I've operated here.

Ambiguous Scope

When the problem is clear but the solution isn't. I'm comfortable driving from "we need something better" to a scoped, shipped product.

Greenfield in Legacy

Building new systems inside an existing environment. I keep what's working and build around it.

Cross-Functional Teams

Translating between engineering, operations, and executive stakeholders. Making technical tradeoffs legible to non-technical decision-makers.

Tight Timelines

When weeks matter more than quarters. I scope aggressively, cut what doesn't serve the outcome, and ship something real. Then iterate.

The Differentiator

The Cross-Functional Edge

I can run a DMAIC, document a process to Six Sigma standards, and deploy a FastAPI backend in the same week. That's not a common combination.

A Six Sigma Black Belt is a discipline for thinking about process. It means approaching a broken workflow the same way you'd approach a broken query: measure, identify root cause, test a fix, validate.

The result is automation aimed at root causes. Fix the broken process first, then automate it.
